Algeria.text: Algeria scraped through the group stage in the most dramatic of fashions — a 3-3 draw with Austria that lurched through lead changes before Mahrez's equaliser deep in stoppage time was immediately answered by Kalajdzic at the other end, rendering it a point that felt both earned and fortunate. They advance as one of the best third-placed sides, but that rollercoaster finale hardly screams conviction. Switzerland await in the Round of 32 on Thu Jul 2 20:00 PT, a compact, disciplined European side that will test Algeria's leaky defence far more coldly than Austria did. Survive that, and Colombia or Ghana could follow — before the draw opens towards a potential quarterfinal date with Argentina.

Argentina.text: Argentina swept through Group J without dropping a point, and their 3-1 dismissal of Jordan was a performance of controlled authority — two goals before half-time, Jordan's consolation barely a footnote before Messi added a third with ten minutes remaining to seal it cleanly. Cape Verde arrive on Fri Jul 3 15:00 PT for a Round of 32 tie that carries the look of a formality: the Africans are a spirited outfit, but Argentina are world champions in full stride and firm favourites to advance without serious alarm. Should they, Australia or Egypt likely await in the Round of 16, before a quarterfinal bracket that could produce Algeria or Colombia — and, deeper still, the spectre of Brazil or England in the semifinal.

Australia.text: A goalless draw with Paraguay to close the group stage — cautious, tight, and ultimately sufficient — secured Australia second place in Group D without ever threatening to ignite. Egypt await in the Round of 32 on Fri Jul 3 11:00 PT, a fixture that reads as a genuine contest between two evenly matched sides rather than a foregone conclusion, with Australia's defensive solidity tested against opponents who carry real threat on the break. Survive that, and either Argentina or Cape Verde loom in the Round of 16 — the former a daunting step up in class, the latter a more navigable path deeper into a draw that could eventually produce a quarterfinal collision with Colombia or Switzerland.

Austria.text: Austria scraped through their group in the most dramatic fashion possible, leading Algeria 3-2 in stoppage time only for Mahrez to equalise at 90+3' — before Kalajdzic's reply three minutes later sealed a frantic 3-3 draw that was anything but comfortable. That finish to the group stage, however, lands them directly in front of Spain in the Round of 32 on Thu Jul 2 12:00 PT, a fixture that represents a steep ask for a side that just conceded six group-stage goals — the Spanish will arrive as clear favourites. Survive that, and Croatia or Portugal await in the Round of 16, with Belgium a potential quarterfinal opponent should Austria's unlikely march continue.

Belgium.text: Belgium closed the group stage in emphatic fashion, dismantling New Zealand 5-1 with Trossard helping himself to a brace before De Bruyne and Lukaku added their signatures to a performance that never felt in doubt. They meet Senegal in the Round of 32 on Wed Jul 1 13:00 PT — a genuinely competitive assignment against an athletically formidable side, though Belgium's quality makes them clear favourites to advance. Survive that, and Bosnia & Herzegovina or the United States await in the last sixteen, with the quarterfinal potentially bringing Portugal or Spain into view — the kind of opposition that would offer the sternest measure yet of whether this Belgian generation can finally go deep when it matters.

Bosnia & Herzegovina.text: Bosnia & Herzegovina closed the group stage with genuine authority, going two goals up inside 34 minutes against Qatar and, despite a brief wobble before half-time, seeing the job out through Mahmic's late third. They advance as a best third-placed side, which means their reward is a Round of 32 date with the host nation — USA, on Wed Jul 1 17:00 PT — an assignment that tilts heavily in the Americans' favour on home soil, where crowd and occasion will count for plenty against a Bosnian side that has shown threat but also conceded six. Survive that, and Belgium or Senegal await in the Round of 16, with Portugal or Spain potentially looming in the quarterfinal.

Brazil.text: Brazil came through their Round of 32 tie against Japan the hard way — trailing to Sano's opener before Casemiro levelled midway through the second half, then relying on Martinelli to settle nerves in the fifth minute of stoppage time. A wobble, not a crisis, but not the commanding display the five-time champions would have wanted. Sunday's Round of 16 at 13:00 PT brings either Ivory Coast or Norway, a tie Brazil enter as clear favourites regardless of the opponent, though neither side will travel to simply make up the numbers. Should they progress, the quarterfinal could present stiffer resistance — England or Mexico lurk in that section — with Argentina a possible shadow on the semifinal horizon.

Canada.text: Canada edged past South Africa on the narrowest of margins, Stephen Eustáquio's goal deep into stoppage time sparing them what would have been an agonising round-of-32 exit. Morocco await in the Round of 16 on Sat Jul 4 10:00 PT — a genuine examination, with Walid Regragui's side among the tournament's more dangerous opponents and Canada yet to demonstrate the fluency their attacking depth promises. Survive that, and a quarterfinal on Thu Jul 9 13:00 PT could bring France into view — a collision that would represent the tournament's harshest possible verdict on how far this Canadian generation has truly come.

Cape Verde.text: Cape Verde ground out a goalless draw with Saudi Arabia to close the group stage — three draws from three matches tells its own story of a side built on defensive resilience rather than attacking ambition, qualifying with the minimum required and not a point to spare. That frugality now earns them a Round of 32 appointment with Argentina on Fri Jul 3 15:00 PT, a fixture that represents one of the most daunting assignments the draw could have produced — the world champions are heavy favourites and Cape Verde will need to be everything they have been and more just to compete. Survive that, and Australia or Egypt await in the Round of 16, with a potential quarterfinal against one of Algeria, Colombia, Ghana, or Switzerland the kind of winnable tie the Blue Sharks can dare to imagine from here.

Colombia.text: Colombia closed the group stage with a disciplined, goalless draw against Portugal — a result that speaks to defensive solidity rather than any lack of ambition, and one that secured top spot in Group K with seven points. They face Ghana in the Round of 32 on Fri Jul 3 18:30 PT, a tie Colombia enter as clear favorites; Ghana are capable of disrupting the rhythm of stronger sides but would need a significant upset to advance. Win that, and a Round of 16 meeting with Algeria or Switzerland awaits, before a potential quarterfinal collision with Argentina looms as the tie that could define Colombia's tournament.

Croatia.text: Croatia got through against Ghana the hard way — leading comfortably enough through Sucic's first-half opener, they were pegged back by a Ghanaian equaliser with seventeen minutes left before Vlasic steadied nerves and settled matters seven minutes from time. That late wobble will concern Zlatko Dalic as Croatia now face Portugal in the Round of 32 on Thu Jul 2 16:00 PT, a formidable assignment against one of Europe's elite sides that places Croatia firmly in the underdog bracket. Should they produce an upset, Austria or Spain await in the Round of 16, with France or Morocco lurking as potential semifinal opponents further down a draw that offers no shelter.

Curaçao.text: Curaçao closed their group stage campaign with a 0-2 defeat to Ivory Coast, Nicolas Pépé striking early and then again just past the hour to make it a controlled, comfortable evening for the Elephants. The result confirmed what the tournament had largely borne out across three matches: Curaçao, plucky debutants on the greatest stage, were outgunned at this level. They exit with a single point, a goal difference of minus nine, and the distinction of having competed — if not quite threatened — against top-tier opposition in Group E.

Czech Republic.text: Czech Republic's group stage exit arrived with little ceremony — a 0-3 defeat to Mexico that was effectively settled inside the opening quarter-hour of the second half, Chávez and Quiñones striking within six minutes of each other before Fidalgo's stoppage-time finish applied a flattering coat of finality to what had already been a comfortable Mexican evening. The Czechs finished fourth in Group A with a single point and a goals difference of minus four, scraping through as one of the better third-placed sides before their tournament ended here, emphatically, on the wrong side of a mismatch they could never quite solve.

DR Congo.text: DR Congo ended their group stage with a statement of sorts — Uzbekistan led through Shomurodov after ten minutes, but the Leopards turned the game decisively in the final quarter-hour, Wissa converting from the spot before Mayele and a Wissa second put a flattering gloss on the margin. The reward for advancing as a best third-placed side is a Round of 32 appointment with England on Wed Jul 1 09:00 PT — a formidable assignment by any measure, with the English carrying the weight of expectation that makes them heavy favourites against a side who scraped through the group stage. Survive that, and Ecuador or Mexico await in the Round of 16, before a potential quarterfinal collision with Brazil, Ivory Coast, or Norway that would test the very limits of this Congolese side's ambition.

Ecuador.text: Ecuador came from behind to beat Germany in a result that carries genuine weight — Angulo struck early to level after Sané's second-minute opener, and Plata's 77th-minute winner confirmed a controlled, disciplined performance rather than a smash-and-grab. They advance as a best third-placed side to face Mexico in the Round of 32 on Tue Jun 30 18:00 PT, a neighbourhood derby that figures to be ferocious and even, with neither side holding a clear edge and recent form giving Ecuador every reason for confidence. Survive that, and England or DR Congo await in the Round of 16, before a potential quarterfinal collision with Brazil looms as the draw's most daunting possibility.

Egypt.text: Egypt's group stage ended with a nervy share of the spoils against Iran — Saber's fifth-minute opener was cancelled out inside a quarter-hour by Rezaeian, and neither side could find a winner from there. The Pharaohs qualified in second place, which sets up a Round of 32 meeting with Australia on Fri Jul 3 11:00 PT — a winnable fixture on paper, though the Socceroos are no formality, and Egypt's tendency to concede quickly will need addressing. Survive that, and Argentina could await in the Round of 16, a challenge of an entirely different magnitude.

England.text: England closed out Group L with a controlled if unspectacular 2-0 win over Panama, Bellingham and Kane striking within five minutes of each other midway through the second half to settle any lingering nerves — a professional job rather than a barnstorming one. They meet DR Congo in the Round of 32 on Wed Jul 1 09:00 PT, a tie England will enter as firm favourites, though African opposition at a World Cup has a habit of punishing complacency. Survive that, and Ecuador or Mexico await in the Round of 16, before the draw opens onto a potential quarterfinal collision with Brazil — which would tell us everything we need to know about where this England side actually stands.

France.text: Dembélé dismantled Norway almost single-handedly, completing a first-half hat-trick inside 32 minutes before Aasgaard's brief interruption changed nothing of substance — this was France in full, contemptuous flow, topping Group I with three wins from three. Sweden arrive in the Round of 32 on Tue Jun 30 14:00 PT as a tidy, well-organised side, but France are heavy favourites and should handle them without crisis. Paraguay then wait in the Round of 16 on Sat Jul 4 14:00 PT before the draw opens into more treacherous water — Canada or Morocco lurk in the quarterfinal, and a semifinal could yet produce a collision with Spain, Portugal, or Belgium.

Germany.text: Germany's tournament ends in familiar, painful fashion. They trailed to Enciso's first-half strike, levelled through Havertz after the break, and then surrendered to the shootout — a 3-4 defeat on penalties that felt grimly inevitable once extra time arrived. A group-stage finish of ten goals scored and six points suggested a side with real attacking momentum, yet Paraguay exposed the fragility lurking beneath it, and Germany are out in the Round of 32, bundled out by a team ranked far beneath them on reputation alone.

Ghana.text: Ghana limped out of Group L on the back of a defeat to Croatia that told a familiar story — a late Vlasic goal sealed a 2-1 reverse after Luckassen had briefly levelled in the 73rd minute, the Black Stars snatching hope only to have it extinguished ten minutes later. They advance as a best third-placed team all the same, and face Colombia in the Round of 32 on Fri Jul 3 18:30 PT — a stern examination against a side broadly expected to progress, though Ghana's tournament survival instinct gives them a puncher's chance. Get through that, and Algeria or Switzerland await in the Round of 16, before a potential quarterfinal collision with Argentina looms as the draw's most daunting possibility.

Haiti.text: Haiti's final group game against Morocco ended 4-2, a scoreline that flatters neither side in quite the right way — the Haitians led briefly, equalized before half-time through Isidor, yet conceded four times across a match Morocco ultimately controlled with growing authority. Two late Moroccan goals in the final stages confirmed what the second half had suggested: Haiti were outclassed and outrun. They finish bottom of Group C without a point, their tournament defined by an 8-2 aggregate against them across three matches, and exit a World Cup that exposed the gulf between their ambition and the level required.

Iran.text: Iran's group stage ends with a 1-1 draw against Egypt — a match in which they surrendered the initiative inside five minutes before Rezaeian levelled early in the second quarter, and neither side could force a winner. Three draws across three games told the full story of a tournament in which Iran were never beaten but never convincing, accumulating just enough to squeeze through as one of the best third-placed sides. They exit at the round of 32, undone not by any single defeat but by a persistent inability to turn resistance into results.

Iraq.text: Iraq's group stage ended in humiliation against Senegal, a 5-0 thrashing that was effectively settled inside the first minute and ruthlessly extended across both halves. The Africans scored through five different players, the goals arriving in waves rather than any sustained Iraqi resistance. It was the punctuation mark on a tournament Iraq would sooner forget — three defeats, twelve goals conceded, none scored, and elimination from a competition they technically advanced into as a best third-placed side, only to find the step up in class absolute and unforgiving.

Ivory Coast.text: Nicolas Pépé settled Ivory Coast's group business early and late — a seventh-minute opener and a composed finish just past the hour gave them a clean, unhurried 2-0 win over Curaçao that belied any anxiety about their second-place standing. They meet Norway in the Round of 32 on Tue Jun 30 10:00 PT in what shapes as a genuine contest: Ivory Coast carry real attacking threat but Norway's physicality and set-piece menace make this far from a formality. Survive that, and Brazil await in the Round of 16 — a test of an altogether different order. Should they somehow navigate that, England, Mexico, or Ecuador could loom in the quarterfinal, with Argentina among the possible dangers further still.

Japan.text: Japan held Brazil until the hour mark, Sano's 29th-minute opener giving them genuine belief before Casemiro levelled and Martinelli's stoppage-time winner completed a cruel late twist. That is how their tournament ends — not in a collapse but in heartbreak, undone in the final seconds of a contest they had controlled for long enough to dare to dream. Japan exit the 2026 World Cup with heads held high, their courage and organisation evident throughout a group stage that brought five points and seven goals, but the knockout rounds proved one step too far.

Jordan.text:
Jordan's final group-stage outing against Argentina followed a familiar script: two goals down by the half-hour mark, Al-Tamari pulling one back just after the interval to briefly threaten a comeback, before Messi settled the contest with ten minutes to go. It was a respectable showing in impossible company, but the gulf in class was never seriously in doubt.

Winless across three matches and with a goal difference of minus five, Jordan nonetheless advanced as one of the best third-placed sides — only to exit at that stage, their tournament concluding without a knockout fixture to call their own.

Mexico.text: Mexico closed out Group A in authoritative fashion, dismantling Czech Republic 3-0 with goals arriving in the second half and a late flourish from Fidalgo deep into stoppage time — a performance that announced them as genuine contenders rather than mere participants. They meet Ecuador in the Round of 32 on Tue Jun 30 18:00 PT, a tie Mexico enter as clear favorites: disciplined, already at full throttle, and carrying a perfect group-stage record against a side without comparable momentum. Should they advance, England or DR Congo await in the Round of 16 — a potentially defining test — with Brazil lurking as a quarter-final possibility if the draw opens up that way.

Morocco.text: Morocco scraped past the Netherlands in the most dramatic fashion — trailing to Gakpo's 72nd-minute strike, they levelled through Diop deep into stoppage time and held their nerve to win the shootout 3-2, a result that flattered neither side but rewarded Moroccan resilience handsomely. Canada await in the Round of 16 on Sat Jul 4 10:00 PT, a tie Morocco enter as clear favourites against a host nation still finding its feet at this level. Should they advance, France, Paraguay, or Sweden could provide a considerably sterner quarterfinal examination on Thu Jul 9 13:00 PT — with France representing a genuine test of whether Morocco's Atlas Lions can recapture the giant-killing form that took them to the 2022 semifinal.

Netherlands.text: There's a cruel symmetry to how the Netherlands exited: Gakpo's 72nd-minute strike looked to have settled a tense Round of 32 tie against Morocco, only for Diop to equalise in stoppage time and drag it to a shootout the Dutch lost 2-3. A tournament in which they topped Group F with seven points and a +10 goal difference ended not through any tactical failing but through the lottery of penalties, a brutal conclusion for a side that had looked one of the more convincing teams in the competition.

New Zealand.text: Belgium dismantled New Zealand with something close to contempt, Trossard scoring either side of half-time before De Bruyne and Lukaku twisted the knife — the All Whites' consolation on 84 minutes a cosmetic gesture against a 5-1 final score. New Zealand's tournament is over: they finish fourth in Group G, their single point earned somewhere earlier in the group stage, the gap in class against Europe's elite ultimately impossible to bridge.

Norway.text: France left Norway in no doubt about the gulf in class: Ousmane Dembélé's hat-trick inside 32 minutes turned the group finale into an exercise in damage limitation, and though Aasgaard pulled one back against the tide, a stoppage-time fourth rubbed salt into the wound. Norway advance as Group I runners-up but arrive at the Round of 32 carrying visible scars. They face Ivory Coast on Tue Jun 30 10:00 PT — a winnable assignment on paper, yet one that demands a sharp response in mentality and defensive organisation. Survive that, and Brazil await in the Round of 16, a fixture that would test any side in the tournament; England or Argentina could yet loom still further down the road.

Panama.text: Panama's group stage exit was confirmed with a 0-2 defeat to England, a result that flattered neither side in the opening hour before Bellingham and Kane struck in quick succession midway through the second half to put the tie beyond doubt. Three losses, no points, and a four-goal deficit across Group L tells the full story of a tournament in which Panama never found their footing at this level. They depart having shown that the step up to a 48-team World Cup brought them no easier path to the knockout rounds.

Paraguay.text: Paraguay held Germany to 1-1 through ninety tense minutes — Enciso's first-half strike answered by Havertz just after the break — before nerves of steel in the shootout sent one of the tournament's heavyweight nations home in the Round of 32. That result, a genuine upset by any measure, now earns them a Round of 16 date with either France or Sweden on Sat Jul 4 14:00 PT: France would represent another formidable European giant to slay, while Sweden offers a more navigable but far from straightforward path. Win that, and Canada or Morocco could await in the quarterfinal, with Spain, Portugal, or Brazil lurking as potential semifinal obstacles for a side quickly running out of opponents they were supposed to fear.

Portugal.text: Portugal ground out a goalless draw with Colombia to close their group campaign, a flat, largely uneventful finale that secured second place without any great conviction. They face Croatia in the Round of 32 on Thu Jul 2 16:00 PT — a fixture that carries genuine edge given these sides' recent history at major tournaments, and one in which Portugal enter as moderate favourites but can expect a disciplined, dangerous opponent rather than a comfortable passage. Survive that, and either Austria or the considerable obstacle of Spain awaits in the Round of 16, with Belgium or the United States potentially lurking in the quarterfinal beyond.

Qatar.text: Qatar's group stage exit arrived with little ceremony, a 1-3 defeat to Bosnia & Herzegovina that laid bare the limitations of a side that rarely threatened to trouble the tournament's upper tier. Behind by two goals before half-time following strikes from Alajbegovic and Abunada, Al-Haydos pulled one back on the stroke of the interval to offer a fleeting flicker of hope, only for Mahmic to extinguish it in the 80th minute. The hosts of 2022 finished bottom of Group B and depart with a single point to their name, a tournament record that reflects, honestly enough, where this squad currently stands among the world's best.

Saudi Arabia.text: Saudi Arabia's group stage ended with a goalless draw against Cape Verde that said little for either side — a flat, scoreless contest that confirmed the Green Falcons' limitations rather than dispelling them. Across three matches they managed a single goal, earned two draws, and finished fourth in Group H, advancing only by the grace of the best third-placed team standings. Their tournament ended there: not in a blaze of controversy or misfortune, but in the quiet arithmetic of a side that rarely threatened and ultimately ran out of road.

Scotland.text: Brazil ended Scotland's tournament with something close to contempt — Júnior struck twice before half-time, the second a stoppage-time flourish that summarised the gulf in class, and Cunha added a third after the interval to make it a thorough, joyless afternoon. Scotland had squeezed through as one of the better third-placed sides, a minor achievement rendered hollow by the manner of their exit. They leave the 2026 World Cup having won once in three matches, outscored four goals to one, and ultimately exposed by the first genuine elite opposition they faced.

Senegal.text: Senegal closed the group stage with a statement — a 5-0 dismantling of Iraq that began inside four minutes and kept rolling through a devastating second-half surge, the scoreline flattering to no one. They advance as a best third-placed side, which means the arithmetic was tight elsewhere even if Tuesday's football was not. Belgium await in the Round of 32 on Wed Jul 1 13:00 PT, a genuine examination: the Red Devils carry European pedigree that Senegal's group record of one win from three does not yet match, though a team capable of five unanswered goals is nobody's routine assignment. Survive that, and Portugal or Spain could loom in the quarterfinal.

South Africa.text: A stoppage-time strike from Eustáquio condemned South Africa to a 1-0 defeat by Canada in the Round of 32 — a cruel ending to a match they may well feel they deserved more from, edged out only by a goal that arrived when the draw seemed the likeliest outcome. Their tournament is over, then, not with a collapse but with a wound: eliminated in the last breath of normal time, denied a place in the last sixteen by the narrowest and most punishing of margins.

South Korea.text: South Korea's group stage ended with a 0-1 defeat to South Africa, Maseko's 63rd-minute strike enough to send the Taeguk Warriors out on the wrong note — a flat, uninspiring exit that offered little in mitigation. They had scrapped through as one of the best third-placed sides, but the tournament caught up with them in Atlanta, and a side that never quite found cohesion or cutting edge departed at the first hurdle, their World Cup lasting exactly three matches and yielding just three points.

Spain.text: Spain closed out Group H with a measured 1-0 win over Uruguay, Álex Baena's first-half strike settling a tight contest that the defending European champions controlled without ever needing to shift into a higher gear. Austria arrive for the Round of 32 on Thu Jul 2 12:00 PT as manageable opposition on paper — a disciplined side, but one that few would back to trouble a Spain squad of this quality and organization. Survive that, and the Round of 16 could produce a considerably sterner examination, with Croatia or Portugal — both familiar, sharp rivals — potentially waiting. France or Morocco lurk as possible semifinal obstacles before a final that could yet conjure a meeting with Brazil, Argentina, or England.

Sweden.text: Sweden edged through Group F's final round in unconvincing fashion, conceding to Maeda before Elanga's equaliser six minutes later salvaged a 1-1 draw with Japan — enough to scrape through as one of the best third-placed sides. France await in the Round of 32 on Tue Jun 30 14:00 PT, and few would back Sweden here: this is a stern examination against one of the tournament's genuine contenders, and the Swedes' goal difference and inconsistent group form offer little reason for confidence. Survive that, and Paraguay stand in the Round of 16; beyond them, Canada or Morocco could emerge as quarterfinal opponents with a path potentially opening toward the semifinals.

Switzerland.text: Switzerland finished their group campaign in purposeful fashion, turning a goalless first half against Canada into a controlled second-half performance — Vargas and Manzambi scoring in quick succession after the interval before David's late consolation added a flicker of tension to what was otherwise a composed victory. Group B winners, they meet Algeria in the Round of 32 on Thu Jul 2 20:00 PT, a fixture Switzerland will approach as clear favourites against a side rarely considered among the tournament's upper tier. Survive that, and Colombia or Ghana await in the Round of 16 on Tue Jul 7 13:00 PT, before a potential quarterfinal on Sat Jul 11 18:00 PT that could bring Argentina into view — the point at which Switzerland's tournament would face its sternest examination yet.

Tunisia.text:
There are a few data issues worth flagging before writing this blurb.

The `status` field says Tunisia "advanced as a best third-placed team out of Group F," but `finish` says "4th place," `eliminated` is true, and the record shows 0 points from three losses — a team that cannot have advanced. The `last_match` also has an apparent error: "Skhiri 3'" and "Hecke 62'" appear in `opponent_goals` (Netherlands), but Skhiri and Hecke are not Dutch players — Skhiri is Tunisian, and Hecke is associated with Belgian/Dutch club football but the own-goal or misattribution here is unclear. Most strikingly, one of Tunisia's listed `opponent_goals` is likely a Tunisian own goal or a data-entry mix-up.

I don't want to invent a clean narrative around contradictory facts. Could you clarify:

1. Did Tunisia finish 4th and exit, or did they genuinely advance?
2. Are the goal attributions correct — specifically, were Skhiri and Hecke own goals credited to Netherlands, or are they misplaced in the wrong scorer list?

Once the brief is consistent, I'll write the blurb accurately.

Turkey.text: Turkey's group stage exit arrived with the cruel drama their tournament deserved — a 3-2 win over the United States that was ultimately rendered meaningless in the standings. The Americans struck first through Trusty inside three minutes, yet Güler and Yilmaz turned things around before half-time, only for Berhalter to level early in the second half; Ayhan's stoppage-time header deep into added time sealed the points but not progress. Turkey finished fourth in Group D, their one victory coming too late and too narrow in goal difference to carry them through, and so a tournament of intermittent promise ends without a knockout stage to show for it.

USA.text: The USA closed out the group stage with a bruising 3-2 defeat to Turkey — Trusty's early opener was quickly swallowed up, and a second-half Berhalter goal proved little more than consolation as Ayhan's stoppage-time strike twisted the knife — yet the Americans had already done enough to top Group D. Bosnia & Herzegovina await in the Round of 32 on Wed Jul 1 17:00 PT, a winnable assignment for a side of the USA's stature despite the ragged finish here, though Haris Džaković's generation has shown it can hurt complacent opponents. Survive that, and Belgium or Senegal loom in the Round of 16, with the quarterfinal potentially delivering a heavyweight reckoning against Portugal or Spain.

Uruguay.text: Uruguay's group stage ended with a narrow 0-1 defeat to Spain, Baena's first-half strike proving the solitary difference in a contest that felt tighter than the scoreline suggests — a game Uruguay never quite unlocked despite staying in it until the final whistle. They leave the tournament having advanced as one of the best third-placed teams, only to find no road beyond the group: two draws and a single defeat across three matches tell the story of a side that ground out points without ever imposing themselves, their tournament concluding in the group stage of World Cup 2026.

Uzbekistan.text: Uzbekistan signed off from their maiden World Cup with a 1-3 defeat to DR Congo that told a familiar story: an early Shomurodov strike offered fleeting promise, before the Congolese took control with a penalty from Wissa, a Mayele effort, and a late third that confirmed the margin was entirely deserved. They finish bottom of Group K without a point, having conceded eleven goals across three matches — a brutal education at football's highest level. Central Asia's standard-bearers depart having shown courage simply in qualifying, but the gap in class exposed here gives the next generation a clear-eyed reckoning to build from.
